parents.father_research_notes: Comfort Starr Mygatt of Danbury Conn. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #310.
parents.mother_research_notes: Polly, his wife (full name not given in source).

narrative_blocks.header: Demanded fifty dollars for a new bonnet west
narrative_blocks.body: Before the 1806 wagon journey to Ohio, Polly demanded fifty dollars from her father Comfort Starr Mygatt for a new bonnet, silverware, and linen — over his objection that she would only need 'two tin cups and as many pewter spoons' for mush and milk. She got her fifty dollars; how she spent them is not recorded.

parents.father_research_notes: Comfort Starr Mygatt of Danbury Conn. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #310.
parents.mother_research_notes: Polly, his wife (full name not given in source).

narrative_blocks.header: Demanded fifty dollars for a new bonnet west
narrative_blocks.body: Before the 1806 wagon journey to Ohio, Polly demanded fifty dollars from her father Comfort Starr Mygatt for a new bonnet, silverware, and linen — over his objection that she would only need 'two tin cups and as many pewter spoons' for mush and milk. She got her fifty dollars; how she spent them is not recorded.

parents.father_research_notes: John Whittlesey (Whittlesey book #123, b.1741 Newington CT, d.1812)
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Beale Whittlesey (dau. of Matthew Beale)

narrative_blocks.header: Famous 180-mile midnight ride through frozen Black Swamp
narrative_blocks.body: In February 1813 Maj. Whittlesey rode 180 miles in three days and nights from the Maumee Rapids to Chillicothe with a battlefield message for the Ohio legislature, traveling at night through the flooded and frozen Black Swamp to avoid Indian scouts. The War of 1812 records carry a battle entry in his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Watch dog of the treasury under three presidents
narrative_blocks.body: Taylor appointed Whittlesey first Comptroller of the U.S. Treasury in 1849; Pierce refused his resignation; Lincoln re-appointed him in 1861. Across three presidents he was known as the 'watch dog of the treasury' for refusing to approve any irregular expenditure, holding the post until his death in January 1863.

narrative_blocks.header: Closed law books on Hull's August 1812 surrender
narrative_blocks.body: When news of Hull's August 1812 surrender of Detroit reached Canfield, Whittlesey closed his law books, dismissed his clients, and was on the road to Cleveland the second day after as aide to Maj.-Gen. Wadsworth. He served the war as Brigade Major in the Army of the Northwest under Gen. William Henry Harrison.

narrative_blocks.header: Sixteen years in Congress, friend of Henry Clay
narrative_blocks.body: Whittlesey served the U.S. Congress continuously from 1822 to 1838, sixteen years, chairing the Committee of Claims. He was a founder of the Whig Party and an ardent friend of Henry Clay.

narrative_blocks.header: Solar eclipse darkened the morning after Pittsburgh
narrative_blocks.body: On the morning after leaving Pittsburgh in June 1806, the Whittleseys were surprised to find darkness spreading over the land — the total solar eclipse of June 16, 1806. They reached Canfield Mahoning County Ohio eleven days later, his first home as the Western Reserve's public prosecutor.

narrative_blocks.header: First public prosecutor of the Western Reserve
narrative_blocks.body: Within his first year in Canfield, Whittlesey was appointed public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve in 1807 — a region that ran the northern third of Ohio. The work paid the new family's bills and built his practice into one of the strongest legal firms in the West.

cross_connections.display_label: Twenty-year law partnership — Whittlesey & Newton, Canfield OH 1821-1841
Tallmadge bought the land; Whittlesey prosecuted its cases
Mentored cousin Frederick at Canfield law office
cross_connections.notes: Hon. Frederick Whittlesey (X02788, Whittlesey #650, Cleveland OH judge) studied law for one year (~1827) at Elisha's Whittlesey & Newton office in Canfield Ohio before opening his own Elyria practice. Documented in Cleveland Herald obituary Nov 22, 1854.

bio.title: Honorable
bio.photo_notes: First Comptroller of the U.S. Treasury under Taylor, Pierce, and Lincoln; 'watch dog of the treasury'.

military_service.unit: Ohio Militia (Captain 1808; aide-de-camp to Maj.-Gen. Wadsworth 1810); Army of the Northwest under Gen. Harrison (Brigade Major 1812-1813)
military_service.notes: Closed law books on news of Hull's August 1812 surrender; on road to Cleveland second day after as Wadsworth's aide. Famous 180-mile ride February 1813 from Maumee Rapids to Chillicothe (3 days/nights through flooded and frozen Black Swamp at night to avoid Indian scouts) carrying messages from Army of the Northwest after Winchester's defeat to the Ohio legislature in session. Contracted camp fever at Fort Meigs; nursed by Rev. Joseph Badger and Dr. Stonard of the Virginia line. Returned home March 1813. Battle entry WAR009 BTL041 'Whittlesey's Ride — Maumee Rapids to Chillicothe' is named for him.

career.title: Public Prosecutor
career.notes: First public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve.

career.title: Captain
career.notes: Promoted to captaincy 1808; commissioned aide-de-camp to Maj.-Gen. Wadsworth, 4th Division Ohio Militia, 1810.

career.title: Brigade Major
career.notes: Under President Madison's call, Brigade Major under Gen. William Henry Harrison. Contracted camp fever at Fort Meigs; nursed back by Rev. Joseph Badger and Dr. Stonard. Returned home March 1813.

career.title: U.S. Representative
career.notes: Sixteen continuous years. Chairman of the Committee of Claims. Founder of the Whig Party; close friend of Henry Clay.

career.title: Auditor for the Treasury for the Post-office Department
career.notes: Appointed by President Harrison 1841; resigned September 1843.

career.title: First Comptroller of the U.S. Treasury
career.notes: Appointed by President Taylor May 1849. Continued under Pierce who refused his resignation. Resigned at start of Pierce administration but Pierce reinstated him to close of administration. Returned Canfield 1857; re-appointed by President Lincoln 1861. Held office until death Jan 7, 1863. Known as 'watch dog of the treasury.'
